[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Cello%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Cello%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Cello%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Cello%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(January 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

([/ˌvaɪələnˈtʃɛloʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*VY-ə-lən-CHEL-oh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), Italian pronunciation: [[vjolonˈtʃɛllo]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")),[[1]](#cite_note-1) commonly abbreviated as **cello** ([/ˈtʃɛloʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Cello.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Cello.wav") [*CHEL-oh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), is a medium-low pitched bowed [string instrument](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument") of the [violin family](/wiki/Violin_family "Violin family"). Its four strings are usually [tuned](/wiki/Intonation_(music) "Intonation (music)") in [perfect fifths](/wiki/Perfect_fifth "Perfect fifth"): from low to high, [C2, G2, D3 and A3](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation"). The [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola")'s four strings are each an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") higher. Music for the cello is generally written in the [bass clef](/wiki/Bass_clef "Bass clef"); the [tenor clef](/wiki/Tenor_clef "Tenor clef") and [treble clef](/wiki/Treble_clef "Treble clef") are used for higher-range passages.

or **violoncellist**, the instrument enjoys a large solo repertoire [with](/wiki/Cello_sonata "Cello sonata") and [without accompaniment](/wiki/List_of_solo_cello_pieces "List of solo cello pieces"), as well as numerous [concerti](/wiki/Cello_concerto "Cello concerto"). As a solo instrument, the cello uses its whole range, from bass to soprano, and in [chamber music](/wiki/Chamber_music "Chamber music"), such as [string quartets](/wiki/String_quartet "String quartet") and the [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra")'s [string section](/wiki/String_section "String section"), it often plays the bass part, where it may be reinforced an octave lower by the [double basses](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass"). [Figured bass](/wiki/Figured_bass "Figured bass") music of the [Baroque era](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music") typically assumes a cello, [viola da gamba](/wiki/Viol "Viol") or [bassoon](/wiki/Bassoon "Bassoon") as part of the [basso continuo](/wiki/Basso_continuo "Basso continuo") group alongside chordal instruments such as [organ](/wiki/Pipe_organ "Pipe organ"), [harpsichord](/wiki/Harpsichord "Harpsichord"), [lute](/wiki/Lute "Lute"), or [theorbo](/wiki/Theorbo "Theorbo"). Cellos are found in many other ensembles, from modern [Chinese orchestras](/wiki/Chinese_orchestra "Chinese orchestra") (as a replacement of Gehu [革胡](/wiki/Gehu "Gehu")）to [cello rock](/wiki/Cello_rock "Cello rock") bands.

The **violoncello** ([/ˌvaɪələnˈtʃɛloʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*VY-ə-lən-CHEL-oh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), Italian pronunciation: [[vjolonˈtʃɛllo]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")),[[1]](#cite_note-1) commonly abbreviated as **cello** ([/ˈtʃɛloʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Cello.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Cello.wav") [*CHEL-oh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), is a medium-low pitched bowed [string instrument](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument") of the [violin family](/wiki/Violin_family "Violin family"). Its four strings are usually [tuned](/wiki/Intonation_(music) "Intonation (music)") in [perfect fifths](/wiki/Perfect_fifth "Perfect fifth"): from low to high, [C2, G2, D3 and A3](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation"). The [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola")'s four strings are each an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") higher. Music for the cello is generally written in the [bass clef](/wiki/Bass_clef "Bass clef"); the [tenor clef](/wiki/Tenor_clef "Tenor clef") and [treble clef](/wiki/Treble_clef "Treble clef") are used for higher-range passages.

Played by a **cellist** or **violoncellist**, the instrument enjoys a large solo repertoire [with](/wiki/Cello_sonata "Cello sonata") and [without accompaniment](/wiki/List_of_solo_cello_pieces "List of solo cello pieces"), as well as numerous [concerti](/wiki/Cello_concerto "Cello concerto"). As a solo instrument, the cello uses its whole range, from bass to soprano, and in [chamber music](/wiki/Chamber_music "Chamber music"), such as [string quartets](/wiki/String_quartet "String quartet") and the [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra")'s [string section](/wiki/String_section "String section"), it often plays the bass part, where it may be reinforced an octave lower by the [double basses](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass"). [Figured bass](/wiki/Figured_bass "Figured bass") music of the [Baroque era](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music") typically assumes a cello, [viola da gamba](/wiki/Viol "Viol") or [bassoon](/wiki/Bassoon "Bassoon") as part of the [basso continuo](/wiki/Basso_continuo "Basso continuo") group alongside chordal instruments such as [organ](/wiki/Pipe_organ "Pipe organ"), [harpsichord](/wiki/Harpsichord "Harpsichord"), [lute](/wiki/Lute "Lute"), or [theorbo](/wiki/Theorbo "Theorbo"). Cellos are found in many other ensembles, from modern [Chinese orchestras](/wiki/Chinese_orchestra "Chinese orchestra") (as a replacement of Gehu [革胡](/wiki/Gehu "Gehu")）to [cello rock](/wiki/Cello_rock "Cello rock") bands.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name *cello* is derived from the ending of the [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") *violoncello*,[[2]](#cite_note-2) which means "little [violone](/wiki/Violone "Violone")". [Violone](/wiki/Violone "Violone") ("big viola") was a large-sized member of [viol](/wiki/Viol "Viol") (viola da gamba) family or the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") ([viola da braccio](/wiki/Viola_da_braccio "Viola da braccio")) family. The term "violone" today usually refers to the lowest-pitched instrument of the viols, a family of stringed instruments that went out of fashion around the end of the 17th century in most countries except England and, especially, France, where they survived another half-century before the louder [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") family came into greater favour in that country as well.

In modern [symphony orchestras](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra"), it is the second largest stringed instrument (the [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass") is the largest). Thus, the name "violoncello" contained both the [augmentative](/wiki/Augmentative "Augmentative") "*-one*" ("big") and the [diminutive](/wiki/Diminutive "Diminutive") "*-cello*" ("little"). By the turn of the 20th century, it had become common to shorten the name to 'cello, with the apostrophe indicating the missing stem.[[3]](#cite_note-countess-3) It is now customary to use "cello" without apostrophe as the full designation.[[3]](#cite_note-countess-3) *Viol* is derived from the root *[viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola")*, which was derived from [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") *vitula*, meaning stringed instrument.

## General description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: General description")]

### Tuning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Tuning")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Cello_study.jpg/250px-Cello_study.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cello_study.jpg)

Cello close-up

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Violoncello_open_strings.png/250px-Violoncello_open_strings.png)](/wiki/File:Violoncello_open_strings.png)

Cello open strings [Play](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/d/d4/Violoncello_open_strings_actual.mid/Violoncello_open_strings_actual.mid.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Violoncello_open_strings_actual.mid "File:Violoncello open strings actual.mid")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Cello_and_a_bow.jpg/250px-Cello_and_a_bow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cello_and_a_bow.jpg)

Cello close-up with a bow.

Cellos are tuned in [fifths](/wiki/Perfect_fifth "Perfect fifth"), starting with [C2](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation") (two [octaves](/wiki/Octave "Octave") below [middle C](/wiki/Middle_C "Middle C")), followed by G2, D3, and then A3. It is tuned in the exact same intervals and strings as the [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola"), but an octave lower. Similar to the [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass"), the cello has an [endpin](/wiki/Endpin "Endpin") that rests on the floor to support the instrument's weight. The cello is most closely associated with [European classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music"). The instrument is a part of the standard [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra"), as part of the [string section](/wiki/String_section "String section"), and is the bass voice of the [string quartet](/wiki/String_quartet "String quartet") (although many composers give it a melodic role as well), as well as being part of many other [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_music "Chamber music") groups.

### Works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Works")]

Among the most well-known Baroque works for the cello are [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach")'s six unaccompanied [Suites](/wiki/Cello_Suites_(Bach) "Cello Suites (Bach)"). Other significant works include Sonatas and Concertos by [Antonio Vivaldi](/wiki/Antonio_Vivaldi "Antonio Vivaldi"), and solo sonatas by [Francesco Geminiani](/wiki/Francesco_Geminiani "Francesco Geminiani") and [Giovanni Bononcini](/wiki/Giovanni_Bononcini "Giovanni Bononcini"). [Domenico Gabrielli](/wiki/Domenico_Gabrielli "Domenico Gabrielli") was one of the first composers to treat the cello as a solo instrument. As a [basso continuo](/wiki/Figured_bass "Figured bass") instrument the cello may have been used in works by [Francesca Caccini](/wiki/Francesca_Caccini "Francesca Caccini") (1587–1641), [Barbara Strozzi](/wiki/Barbara_Strozzi "Barbara Strozzi") (1619–1677) with pieces such as *Il primo libro di madrigali, per 2–5 voci e basso continuo, op. 1* and [Elisabeth Jacquet de La Guerre](/wiki/%C3%89lisabeth_Jacquet_de_La_Guerre "Élisabeth Jacquet de La Guerre") (1665–1729), who wrote six sonatas for violin and basso continuo. [Francesco Supriani](/wiki/Francesco_Paolo_Supriani "Francesco Paolo Supriani")'s *Principij da imparare a suonare il violoncello e con 12 Toccate a solo* (before 1753), an early manual for learning the cello, dates from this era. As the title of the work suggests, it contains 12 toccatas for solo cello, which along with Johann Sebastian Bach's Cello Suites, are some of the first works of that type.[[4]](#cite_note-VWalden2004-4)

From the [Classical era](/wiki/Classical_music_era "Classical music era"), the two concertos by [Joseph Haydn](/wiki/Joseph_Haydn "Joseph Haydn") in [C major](/wiki/Cello_Concerto_No._1_(Haydn) "Cello Concerto No. 1 (Haydn)") and [D major](/wiki/Cello_Concerto_No._2_(Haydn) "Cello Concerto No. 2 (Haydn)") stand out, as do the five sonatas for cello and pianoforte of [Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven"), which span the important three periods of his compositional evolution. Other outstanding examples include the three Concerti by [Carl Philipp Emanuel Bach](/wiki/Carl_Philipp_Emanuel_Bach "Carl Philipp Emanuel Bach"), Capricci by dall'Abaco, and Sonatas by Flackton, Boismortier, and [Luigi Boccherini](/wiki/Luigi_Boccherini "Luigi Boccherini"). A *Divertimento for Piano, Clarinet, Viola and Cello* is among the surviving works by [Duchess Anna Amalia of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel](/wiki/Duchess_Anna_Amalia_of_Brunswick-Wolfenb%C3%BCttel "Duchess Anna Amalia of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel") (1739–1807). [Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart") supposedly wrote a Cello Concerto in F major, K. 206a in 1775, but this has since been lost. His [Sinfonia Concertante in A major, K. 320e](/wiki/Sinfonia_Concertante_for_Violin,_Viola,_Cello_and_Orchestra_(Mozart) "Sinfonia Concertante for Violin, Viola, Cello and Orchestra (Mozart)") includes a solo part for cello, along with the violin and viola, although this work is incomplete and only exists in fragments, therefore it is given an Anhang number (Anh. 104).

Well-known works of the [Romantic era](/wiki/Romantic_music_era "Romantic music era") include the [Robert Schumann](/wiki/Robert_Schumann "Robert Schumann") [Concerto](/wiki/Cello_Concerto_(Schumann) "Cello Concerto (Schumann)"), the [Antonín Dvořák](/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k "Antonín Dvořák") [Concerto](/wiki/Cello_Concerto_(Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k) "Cello Concerto (Dvořák)"), the first [Camille Saint-Saëns](/wiki/Camille_Saint-Sa%C3%ABns "Camille Saint-Saëns") [Concerto](/wiki/Cello_Concerto_No._1_(Saint-Sa%C3%ABns) "Cello Concerto No. 1 (Saint-Saëns)"), as well as the two sonatas and the [Double Concerto](/wiki/Double_Concerto_(Brahms) "Double Concerto (Brahms)") by [Johannes Brahms](/wiki/Johannes_Brahms "Johannes Brahms"). A review of compositions for cello in the Romantic era must include the German composer [Fanny Hensel](/wiki/Fanny_Hensel "Fanny Hensel"), who wrote Fantasia in G Minor for cello and piano[[5]](#cite_note-5) and a Capriccio in A-flat for cello.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Compositions from the late 19th and early 20th century include three cello sonatas (including the Cello Sonata in C Minor written in 1880) by [Dame Ethel Smyth](/wiki/Ethel_Smyth "Ethel Smyth") (1858–1944), [Edward Elgar](/wiki/Edward_Elgar "Edward Elgar")'s [Cello Concerto in E minor](/wiki/Cello_Concerto_(Elgar) "Cello Concerto (Elgar)"), [Claude Debussy](/wiki/Claude_Debussy "Claude Debussy")'s [Sonata for Cello and Piano](/wiki/Cello_Sonata_(Debussy) "Cello Sonata (Debussy)"), and unaccompanied cello sonatas by [Zoltán Kodály](/wiki/Zolt%C3%A1n_Kod%C3%A1ly "Zoltán Kodály") and [Paul Hindemith](/wiki/Paul_Hindemith "Paul Hindemith"). Pieces including cello were written by [American Music Center](/wiki/American_Music_Center "American Music Center") founder [Marion Bauer](/wiki/Marion_Bauer "Marion Bauer") (1882–1955) (two trio sonatas for flute, cello, and piano) and [Ruth Crawford Seeger](/wiki/Ruth_Crawford_Seeger "Ruth Crawford Seeger") (1901–1953) (Diaphonic suite No. 2 for bassoon and cello).

The cello's versatility made it popular with many composers in this era, such as [Sergei Prokofiev](/wiki/Sergei_Prokofiev "Sergei Prokofiev"), [Dmitri Shostakovich](/wiki/Dmitri_Shostakovich "Dmitri Shostakovich"), [Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten"), [György Ligeti](/wiki/Gy%C3%B6rgy_Ligeti "György Ligeti"), [Witold Lutoslawski](/wiki/Witold_Lutoslawski "Witold Lutoslawski") and [Henri Dutilleux](/wiki/Henri_Dutilleux "Henri Dutilleux"). Polish composer [Grażyna Bacewicz](/wiki/Gra%C5%BCyna_Bacewicz "Grażyna Bacewicz") (1909–1969) was writing for cello in the mid 20th century with Concerto No. 1 for Cello and Orchestra (1951), Concerto No. 2 for Cello and Orchestra (1963) and in 1964 composed her Quartet for four cellos.

Today it is sometimes featured in [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") and [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") recordings, examples of which are noted later in this article. The cello has also appeared in major [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") and [R & B](/wiki/R_%26_B "R & B") performances, such as singers [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") and [Ne-Yo](/wiki/Ne-Yo "Ne-Yo")'s 2007 performance at the [American Music Awards](/wiki/American_Music_Awards "American Music Awards").[[7]](#cite_note-7) The instrument has also been modified for [Indian classical music](/wiki/Indian_classical_music "Indian classical music") by Nancy Lesh and [Saskia Rao-de Haas](/wiki/Saskia_Rao-de_Haas "Saskia Rao-de Haas").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

The [violin family](/wiki/Viola_da_braccio "Viola da braccio"), including cello-sized instruments, emerged c. 1500 as a family of instruments distinct from the [viola da gamba](/wiki/Viol "Viol") family. The earliest depictions of the violin family, from Italy c. 1530, show three sizes of instruments, roughly corresponding to what we now call [violins](/wiki/Violin "Violin"), [violas](/wiki/Viola "Viola"), and cellos. Contrary to a popular misconception, the cello did not evolve from the viola da gamba, but existed alongside it for about two and a half centuries. The violin family is also known as the viola da braccio (meaning viola for the arm) family, a reference to the primary way the members of the family are held. This is to distinguish it from the viola da gamba (meaning viola for the leg) family, in which all the members are all held with the legs. The likely predecessors of the violin family include the [lira da braccio](/wiki/Lira_da_braccio "Lira da braccio") and the [rebec](/wiki/Rebec "Rebec"). The earliest surviving cellos are made by [Andrea Amati](/wiki/Andrea_Amati "Andrea Amati"), the first known member of the celebrated [Amati](/wiki/Amati "Amati") family of [luthiers](/wiki/Luthier "Luthier").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The direct ancestor to the violoncello was the [bass violin](/wiki/Bass_violin "Bass violin").[*unt. library*] Monteverdi referred to the instrument as "basso de viola da braccio" in *Orfeo* (1607). Although the first [bass violin](/wiki/Bass_violin "Bass violin"), possibly invented as early as 1538, was most likely inspired by the viol, it was created to be used in consort with the violin. The bass violin was actually often referred to as a "*violone*", or "large viola", as were the viols of the same period. Instruments that share features with both the bass violin and the *viola da gamba* appear in Italian art of the early 16th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Cello_strung_gut.JPG/250px-Cello_strung_gut.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cello_strung_gut.JPG)

A baroque cello strung with gut strings. Note the absence of fine tuners on the tailpiece.

Around 1700, Italian players popularized the cello in northern Europe, although the bass violin (basse de violon) continued to be used for another two decades in France.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Many existing bass violins were literally cut down in size to convert them into cellos according to the smaller pattern developed by [Stradivarius](/wiki/Stradivarius "Stradivarius"), who also made a number of old pattern large cellos (the 'Servais').[[10]](#cite_note-vrzuok-10) The sizes, names, and tunings of the cello varied widely by geography and time.[[10]](#cite_note-vrzuok-10) The size was not standardized until c. 1750.

Despite similarities to the [viola da gamba](/wiki/Viol "Viol"), the cello is actually part of the [viola da braccio](/wiki/Viola_da_braccio "Viola da braccio") family, meaning "viol of the arm", which includes, among others, the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") and [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola"). Though paintings like [Bruegel](/wiki/Jan_Brueghel_the_Elder "Jan Brueghel the Elder")'s "The Rustic Wedding", and [Jambe de Fer](/wiki/Philibert_Jambe_de_Fer "Philibert Jambe de Fer") in his *Epitome Musical* suggest that the bass violin had [alternate playing positions](/wiki/Cello_da_spalla "Cello da spalla"), these were short-lived and the more practical and ergonomic *a gamba* position eventually replaced them entirely.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Josephine_van_Lier_plays_violoncello_piccolo.jpg/250px-Josephine_van_Lier_plays_violoncello_piccolo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Josephine_van_Lier_plays_violoncello_piccolo.jpg)

[Josephine van Lier](/wiki/Josephine_van_Lier "Josephine van Lier") plays a violoncello piccolo, a five-string variant of the Baroque cello, during a recording session of [Bach's 6th Cello Suite](/wiki/Cello_Suites_(Bach)#Suite_No._6_in_D_major,_BWV_1012 "Cello Suites (Bach)").

[Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque")-era cellos differed from the modern instrument in several ways. The neck has a different form and angle, which matches the baroque bass-bar and stringing.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The fingerboard is usually shorter than that of the modern cello, as the highest notes are not often called for in baroque music. Modern cellos have an endpin at the bottom to support the instrument (and transmit some of the sound through the floor),[[13]](#cite_note-13) while Baroque cellos are held only by the calves of the player. Modern bows curve in and are held at the [frog](/wiki/Bow_frog "Bow frog"); Baroque bows curve out and are held closer to the bow's point of balance. Modern strings are normally [flatwound](/wiki/Flatwound "Flatwound") with a metal (or synthetic) core; Baroque strings are made of [gut](/wiki/Catgut "Catgut"), with the G and C strings wire-wound. Modern cellos often have fine tuners connecting the strings to the tailpiece, which makes it much easier to tune the instrument, but such pins are rendered ineffective by the flexibility of the gut strings used on Baroque cellos. Overall, the modern instrument has much higher string tension than the Baroque cello,[[14]](#cite_note-14) resulting in a louder, more projecting tone, with fewer overtones. In addition, the instrument was less standardized in size and number of strings; a smaller, five-string variant (the violoncello piccolo) was commonly used as a solo instrument and five-string instruments are occasionally specified in the Baroque repertoire. BWV 1012 (Bach's 6th Cello Suite) was written for 5-string cello. The additional high E string on the five-string cello is an octave below the same string on the Violin, so anything written for the violin can be played on the 5 string cello, sounding an octave lower than written.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Few educational works specifically devoted to the cello existed before the 18th century and those that do exist contain little value to the performer beyond simple accounts of instrumental technique. One of the earliest cello manuals is [Michel Corrette](/wiki/Michel_Corrette "Michel Corrette")'s *Méthode, thèorique et pratique pour apprendre en peu de temps le violoncelle dans sa perfection* (Paris, 1741).[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Orchestral

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Orchestral")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/FHM-Orchestra-mk2006-03.jpg/250px-FHM-Orchestra-mk2006-03.jpg)](/wiki/File:FHM-Orchestra-mk2006-03.jpg)

The cello section of the orchestra of the Munich University of Applied Sciences is shown here.

Cellos are part of the standard [symphony orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra"), which usually includes ten to fourteen cellists. The cello section, in standard orchestral seating, is located on stage left (the audience's right) in the front, opposite the first violin section. However, some orchestras and conductors prefer switching the positioning of the viola and cello sections. The *principal* cellist is the section leader, determining bowings for the section in conjunction with other string principals, playing solos, and leading entrances (when the section begins to play its part). Principal players always sit closest to the audience.

The cellos are a critical part of orchestral music; all symphonic works involve the cello section, and many pieces require cello soli or solos. Much of the time, cellos provide part of the low-register harmony for the orchestra. Often, the cello section plays the melody for a brief period, before returning to the harmony role. There are also cello [concertos](/wiki/Concerto "Concerto"), which are orchestral pieces that feature a solo cellist accompanied by an entire orchestra.

### Solo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Solo")]

There are numerous [cello concertos](/wiki/Cello_concerto "Cello concerto") – where a solo cello is accompanied by an [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") – notably 25 by [Vivaldi](/wiki/Antonio_Vivaldi "Antonio Vivaldi"), 12 by Boccherini, at least three by [Haydn](/wiki/Joseph_Haydn "Joseph Haydn"), three by C. P. E. Bach, two by [Saint-Saëns](/wiki/Camille_Saint-Sa%C3%ABns "Camille Saint-Saëns"), two by Dvořák, and one each by Robert Schumann, [Lalo](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Lalo "Édouard Lalo"), and Elgar. There were also some composers who, while not otherwise cellists,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] did write cello-specific repertoire, such as [Nikolaus Kraft](/wiki/Nikolaus_Kraft "Nikolaus Kraft"), who wrote six cello concertos. [Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven")'s [Triple Concerto](/wiki/Triple_Concerto_(Beethoven) "Triple Concerto (Beethoven)") for Cello, Violin and Piano and [Brahms](/wiki/Johannes_Brahms "Johannes Brahms")' [Double Concerto](/wiki/Double_Concerto_(Brahms) "Double Concerto (Brahms)") for Cello and Violin are also part of the concertante repertoire, although in both cases the cello shares solo duties with at least one other instrument. Moreover, several composers wrote large-scale pieces for cello and orchestra, which are concertos in all but name. Some familiar "concertos" are [Richard Strauss](/wiki/Richard_Strauss "Richard Strauss")' [tone poem](/wiki/Tone_poem "Tone poem") *[Don Quixote](/wiki/Don_Quixote_(Strauss) "Don Quixote (Strauss)")*, [Tchaikovsky](/wiki/Pyotr_Ilyich_Tchaikovsky "Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky")'s *[Variations on a Rococo Theme](/wiki/Variations_on_a_Rococo_Theme "Variations on a Rococo Theme")*, [Bloch](/wiki/Ernest_Bloch "Ernest Bloch")'s *[Schelomo](/wiki/Schelomo "Schelomo")* and [Bruch](/wiki/Max_Bruch "Max Bruch")'s *[Kol Nidrei](/wiki/Kol_Nidrei_(Bruch) "Kol Nidrei (Bruch)")*.

In the 20th century, the cello repertoire grew immensely. This was partly due to the influence of virtuoso cellist Mstislav Rostropovich, who inspired, commissioned, and premiered dozens of new works. Among these, Prokofiev's *[Symphony-Concerto](/wiki/Symphony-Concerto_(Prokofiev) "Symphony-Concerto (Prokofiev)")*, [Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten")'s *[Cello Symphony](/wiki/Cello_Symphony "Cello Symphony")*, the concertos of Shostakovich and Lutosławski as well as Dutilleux's *[Tout un monde lointain...](/wiki/Tout_un_monde_lointain... "Tout un monde lointain...")* have already become part of the standard repertoire. Other major composers who wrote concertante works for him include [Messiaen](/wiki/Olivier_Messiaen "Olivier Messiaen"), [Jolivet](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Jolivet "André Jolivet"), [Berio](/wiki/Luciano_Berio "Luciano Berio"), and [Penderecki](/wiki/Krzysztof_Penderecki "Krzysztof Penderecki"). In addition, [Arnold](/wiki/Malcolm_Arnold "Malcolm Arnold"), [Barber](/wiki/Samuel_Barber "Samuel Barber"), [Glass](/wiki/Philip_Glass "Philip Glass"), [Hindemith](/wiki/Paul_Hindemith "Paul Hindemith"), [Honegger](/wiki/Arthur_Honegger "Arthur Honegger"), Ligeti, [Myaskovsky](/wiki/Nikolai_Myaskovsky "Nikolai Myaskovsky"), Penderecki, [Rodrigo](/wiki/Joaqu%C3%ADn_Rodrigo "Joaquín Rodrigo"), [Villa-Lobos](/wiki/Heitor_Villa-Lobos "Heitor Villa-Lobos") and [Walton](/wiki/William_Walton "William Walton") wrote major [concertos](/wiki/List_of_compositions_for_cello_and_orchestra "List of compositions for cello and orchestra") for other cellists, notably for [Gaspar Cassadó](/wiki/Gaspar_Cassad%C3%B3 "Gaspar Cassadó"), [Aldo Parisot](/wiki/Aldo_Parisot "Aldo Parisot"), Gregor Piatigorsky, [Siegfried Palm](/wiki/Siegfried_Palm "Siegfried Palm") and [Julian Lloyd Webber](/wiki/Julian_Lloyd_Webber "Julian Lloyd Webber").

There are also many [sonatas](/wiki/Cello_sonata "Cello sonata") for cello and [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano"). Those written by [Beethoven](/wiki/Beethoven "Beethoven"), [Mendelssohn](/wiki/Felix_Mendelssohn "Felix Mendelssohn"), [Chopin](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin "Frédéric Chopin"), [Brahms](/wiki/Brahms "Brahms"), [Grieg](/wiki/Edvard_Grieg "Edvard Grieg"), [Rachmaninoff](/wiki/Sergei_Rachmaninoff "Sergei Rachmaninoff"), [Debussy](/wiki/Claude_Debussy "Claude Debussy"), [Fauré](/wiki/Gabriel_Faur%C3%A9 "Gabriel Fauré"), Shostakovich, Prokofiev, [Poulenc](/wiki/Francis_Poulenc "Francis Poulenc"), [Carter](/wiki/Elliott_Carter "Elliott Carter"), and [Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten") are particularly well known.

Other important pieces for cello and piano include Schumann's five *Stücke im Volkston* and transcriptions like [Schubert](/wiki/Franz_Schubert "Franz Schubert")'s *[Arpeggione Sonata](/wiki/Arpeggione_Sonata "Arpeggione Sonata")* (originally for [arpeggione](/wiki/Arpeggione "Arpeggione") and piano), [César Franck](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_Franck "César Franck")'s [Cello Sonata](/wiki/Cello_Sonata_(Franck/Delsart) "Cello Sonata (Franck/Delsart)") (originally a violin sonata, transcribed by [Jules Delsart](/wiki/Jules_Delsart "Jules Delsart") with the composer's approval), [Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky")'s *[Suite italienne](/wiki/Pulcinella_(ballet)#Suite_italienne "Pulcinella (ballet)")* (transcribed by the composer – with Gregor Piatigorsky – from his ballet *Pulcinella*) and [Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók")'s first rhapsody (also transcribed by the composer, originally for violin and piano).

There are pieces for [cello solo](/wiki/List_of_solo_cello_pieces "List of solo cello pieces"), [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach")'s six [Suites for Cello](/wiki/Cello_Suites_(Bach) "Cello Suites (Bach)") (which are among the best-known solo cello pieces), [Kodály](/wiki/Zolt%C3%A1n_Kod%C3%A1ly "Zoltán Kodály")'s [Sonata for Solo Cello](/wiki/Sonata_for_Solo_Cello_(Kod%C3%A1ly) "Sonata for Solo Cello (Kodály)") and Britten's three [Cello Suites](/wiki/Cello_Suites_(Britten) "Cello Suites (Britten)"). Other notable examples include [Hindemith](/wiki/Paul_Hindemith "Paul Hindemith")'s and [Ysaÿe](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Ysa%C3%BFe "Eugène Ysaÿe")'s Sonatas for Solo Cello, Dutilleux's *Trois Strophes sur le Nom de Sacher*, Berio's *Les Mots Sont Allés*, [Cassadó](/wiki/Gaspar_Cassad%C3%B3 "Gaspar Cassadó")'s Suite for Solo Cello, Ligeti's [Solo Sonata](/wiki/Sonata_for_Solo_Cello_(Ligeti) "Sonata for Solo Cello (Ligeti)"), Carter's two *Figment*s and [Xenakis](/wiki/Iannis_Xenakis "Iannis Xenakis")' *[Nomos Alpha](/wiki/Nomos_Alpha "Nomos Alpha")* and *Kottos*.

There are also modern solo pieces written for cello, such as Julie-O by [Mark Summer](/wiki/Mark_Summer "Mark Summer").

### Quartets and other ensembles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Quartets and other ensembles")]

The cello is a member of the traditional [string quartet](/wiki/String_quartet "String quartet") as well as [string quintets](/wiki/String_quintets "String quintets"), [sextet](/wiki/String_sextet "String sextet") or [trios](/wiki/String_trio "String trio") and other mixed ensembles.
There are also pieces written for two, three, four, or more cellos; this type of ensemble is also called a "cello choir" and its sound is familiar from the introduction to [Rossini](/wiki/Rossini "Rossini")'s [William Tell Overture](/wiki/William_Tell_Overture "William Tell Overture") as well as Zaccharia's prayer scene in [Verdi](/wiki/Verdi "Verdi")'s [Nabucco](/wiki/Nabucco "Nabucco"). [Tchaikovsky](/wiki/Tchaikovsky "Tchaikovsky")'s [1812 Overture](/wiki/1812_Overture "1812 Overture") also starts with a cello ensemble, with four cellos playing the top lines and two violas playing the bass lines. As a self-sufficient ensemble, its most famous repertoire is [Heitor Villa-Lobos](/wiki/Heitor_Villa-Lobos "Heitor Villa-Lobos")' first of his [Bachianas Brasileiras](/wiki/Bachianas_Brasileiras "Bachianas Brasileiras") for cello ensemble (the fifth is for soprano and 8 cellos). Other examples are [Offenbach](/wiki/Jacques_Offenbach "Jacques Offenbach")'s cello duets, quartet, and sextet, [Pärt](/wiki/P%C3%A4rt "Pärt")'s [Fratres](/wiki/Fratres "Fratres") for eight cellos and [Boulez](/wiki/Pierre_Boulez "Pierre Boulez")' *[Messagesquisse](/wiki/Messagesquisse "Messagesquisse")* for seven cellos, or even Villa-Lobos' rarely played *Fantasia Concertante* (1958) for 32 cellos. The 12 cellists of the [Berlin Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Berlin_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Berlin Philharmonic Orchestra") (or "the Twelve" as they have since taken to being called) specialize in this repertoire and have commissioned many works, including arrangements of well-known popular songs.

### Popular music, jazz, world music and neoclassical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Popular music, jazz, world music and neoclassical")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Apocalyptica_-_Ilosaarirock_2009.jpg/250px-Apocalyptica_-_Ilosaarirock_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apocalyptica_-_Ilosaarirock_2009.jpg)

[Apocalyptica](/wiki/Apocalyptica "Apocalyptica") at the 2009 [Ilosaarirock](/wiki/Ilosaarirock "Ilosaarirock") festival.

The cello is less common in [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") than in classical music. Several bands feature a cello in their standard line-up, including Hoppy Jones of [the Ink Spots](/wiki/The_Ink_Spots "The Ink Spots") and Joe Kwon of [the Avett Brothers](/wiki/The_Avett_Brothers "The Avett Brothers"). The more common use in [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") and [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") is to bring the instrument in for a particular song. In the 1960s, artists such as [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") and [Cher](/wiki/Cher "Cher") used the cello in popular music, in songs such as The Beatles' "[Yesterday](/wiki/Yesterday_(Beatles_song) "Yesterday (Beatles song)")", "[Eleanor Rigby](/wiki/Eleanor_Rigby "Eleanor Rigby")" and "[Strawberry Fields Forever](/wiki/Strawberry_Fields_Forever "Strawberry Fields Forever")", and Cher's "[Bang Bang (My Baby Shot Me Down)](/wiki/Bang_Bang_(My_Baby_Shot_Me_Down) "Bang Bang (My Baby Shot Me Down)")". "[Good Vibrations](/wiki/Good_Vibrations "Good Vibrations")" by [the Beach Boys](/wiki/The_Beach_Boys "The Beach Boys") includes the cello in its instrumental ensemble, which includes a number of instruments unusual for this sort of music. Bass guitarist [Jack Bruce](/wiki/Jack_Bruce "Jack Bruce"), who had originally studied music on a performance scholarship for cello, played a prominent cello part in "As You Said" on [Cream](/wiki/Cream_(band) "Cream (band)")'s *[Wheels of Fire](/wiki/Wheels_of_Fire "Wheels of Fire")* studio album (1968).

In the 1970s, the [Electric Light Orchestra](/wiki/Electric_Light_Orchestra "Electric Light Orchestra") enjoyed great commercial success taking inspiration from so-called "Beatlesque" arrangements, adding the cello (and violin) to the standard rock combo line-up and in 1978 the UK-based rock band [Colosseum II](/wiki/Colosseum_II "Colosseum II") collaborated with cellist [Julian Lloyd Webber](/wiki/Julian_Lloyd_Webber "Julian Lloyd Webber") on the recording *[Variations](/wiki/Variations_(Andrew_Lloyd_Webber_album) "Variations (Andrew Lloyd Webber album)")*. Most notably, [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd") included a cello solo in their 1970 epic instrumental "[Atom Heart Mother](/wiki/Atom_Heart_Mother_(suite) "Atom Heart Mother (suite)")". Bass guitarist [Mike Rutherford](/wiki/Mike_Rutherford "Mike Rutherford") of [Genesis](/wiki/Genesis_(band) "Genesis (band)") was originally a cellist and included some cello parts in their *[Foxtrot](/wiki/Foxtrot_(album) "Foxtrot (album)")* album.

Established non-traditional cello groups include [Apocalyptica](/wiki/Apocalyptica "Apocalyptica"), a group of Finnish cellists best known for their versions of [Metallica](/wiki/Metallica "Metallica") songs; [Rasputina](/wiki/Rasputina_(band) "Rasputina (band)"), a group of cellists committed to an intricate cello style intermingled with Gothic music; the [Massive Violins](/wiki/Massive_Violins "Massive Violins"), an ensemble of seven singing cellists known for their arrangements of rock, pop and classical hits; Von Cello, a cello-fronted rock power trio; [Break of Reality](/wiki/Break_of_Reality "Break of Reality"), who mix elements of classical music with the more modern rock and metal genre; [Cello Fury](/wiki/Cello_Fury "Cello Fury"), a cello rock band that performs original rock/classical crossover music; and Jelloslave, a Minneapolis-based cello duo with two percussionists. These groups are examples of a style that has become known as [cello rock](/wiki/Cello_rock "Cello rock"). The crossover string quartet [Bond](/wiki/Bond_(band) "Bond (band)") also includes a cellist. Silenzium and Cellissimo Quartet are [Russian](/wiki/Russia "Russia") (Novosibirsk) groups playing rock and metal and having more and more popularity in [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"). [Cold Fairyland](/wiki/Cold_Fairyland "Cold Fairyland") from [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), China is using a cello along with a [pipa](/wiki/Pipa "Pipa") as the main solo instrument to create East meets West progressive (folk) rock.

More recent bands who have used the cello include [Clean Bandit](/wiki/Clean_Bandit "Clean Bandit"), [Aerosmith](/wiki/Aerosmith "Aerosmith"), [The Auteurs](/wiki/The_Auteurs "The Auteurs"), [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)"), [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)"), [Ra Ra Riot](/wiki/Ra_Ra_Riot "Ra Ra Riot"),[[17]](#cite_note-17) [Smashing Pumpkins](/wiki/Smashing_Pumpkins "Smashing Pumpkins"), [James](/wiki/James_(band) "James (band)"), [Talk Talk](/wiki/Talk_Talk "Talk Talk"), [Phillip Phillips](/wiki/Phillip_Phillips "Phillip Phillips"), [OneRepublic](/wiki/OneRepublic "OneRepublic"), [Electric Light Orchestra](/wiki/Electric_Light_Orchestra "Electric Light Orchestra") and the [baroque rock](/wiki/Baroque_rock "Baroque rock") band [Arcade Fire](/wiki/Arcade_Fire "Arcade Fire"). An Atlanta-based trio, King Richard's Sunday Best, also uses a cellist in their lineup. So-called "chamber pop" artists like [Kronos Quartet](/wiki/Kronos_Quartet "Kronos Quartet"), The [Vitamin String Quartet](/wiki/Vitamin_String_Quartet "Vitamin String Quartet") and Margot and the Nuclear So and So's have also recently made cello common in modern alternative rock. Heavy metal band [System of a Down](/wiki/System_of_a_Down "System of a Down") has also made use of the cello's rich sound. The [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") band [The Stiletto Formal](/wiki/The_Stiletto_Formal "The Stiletto Formal") are known for using a cello as a major staple of their sound; similarly, the indie rock band [Canada](/wiki/Canada_(music_group) "Canada (music group)") employs two cello players in their lineup. The orch-rock group [The Polyphonic Spree](/wiki/The_Polyphonic_Spree "The Polyphonic Spree"), which has pioneered the use of stringed and symphonic instruments, employs the cello in creative ways for many of their "[psychedelic](/wiki/Psychedelia "Psychedelia")-esque" melodies. The first-wave [screamo](/wiki/Screamo "Screamo") band I Would Set Myself On Fire For You featured a cello as well as a viola to create a more [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music")-oriented sound. The band [Panic! at the Disco](/wiki/Panic!_at_the_Disco "Panic! at the Disco") uses a cello in their song "Build God, Then We'll Talk", with lead vocalist [Brendon Urie](/wiki/Brendon_Urie "Brendon Urie") recording the cello solo himself. [The Lumineers](/wiki/The_Lumineers "The Lumineers") added cellist Nela Pekarek to the band in 2010. [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead") makes frequent use of cello in their music, notably for the songs "Burn The Witch" and "Glass Eyes" in 2016.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), bassists [Oscar Pettiford](/wiki/Oscar_Pettiford "Oscar Pettiford") and [Harry Babasin](/wiki/Harry_Babasin "Harry Babasin") were among the first to use the cello as a solo instrument; both tuned their instruments in fourths, an octave above the double bass. [Fred Katz](/wiki/Fred_Katz_(cellist) "Fred Katz (cellist)") (who was not a bassist) was one of the first notable jazz cellists to use the instrument's standard tuning and arco technique. Contemporary jazz cellists include [Abdul Wadud](/wiki/Abdul_Wadud_(musician) "Abdul Wadud (musician)"), [Diedre Murray](/wiki/Diedre_Murray "Diedre Murray"), [Ron Carter](/wiki/Ron_Carter "Ron Carter"), [Dave Holland](/wiki/Dave_Holland_(bassist) "Dave Holland (bassist)"), [David Darling](/wiki/David_Darling_(musician) "David Darling (musician)"), [Lucio Amanti](/wiki/Lucio_Amanti "Lucio Amanti"), [Akua Dixon](/wiki/Akua_Dixon "Akua Dixon"), [Ernst Reijseger](/wiki/Ernst_Reijseger "Ernst Reijseger"), [Fred Lonberg-Holm](/wiki/Fred_Lonberg-Holm "Fred Lonberg-Holm"), [Tom Cora](/wiki/Tom_Cora "Tom Cora") and [Erik Friedlander](/wiki/Erik_Friedlander "Erik Friedlander"). Modern musical theatre pieces like Jason Robert Brown's *[The Last Five Years](/wiki/The_Last_Five_Years "The Last Five Years")*, Duncan Sheik's *[Spring Awakening](/wiki/Spring_Awakening_(musical) "Spring Awakening (musical)")*, Adam Guettel's *[Floyd Collins](/wiki/Floyd_Collins_(musical) "Floyd Collins (musical)")*, and Ricky Ian Gordon's *[My Life with Albertine](/wiki/My_Life_with_Albertine "My Life with Albertine")* use small string ensembles (including solo cellos) to a prominent extent.

In Indian classical music, [Saskia Rao-de Haas](/wiki/Saskia_Rao-de_Haas "Saskia Rao-de Haas") is a well-established soloist as well as playing duets with her sitarist husband, Pt. [Shubhendra Rao](/wiki/Shubhendra_Rao "Shubhendra Rao"). Other cellists performing Indian classical music are Nancy Lesh (Dhrupad) and Anup Biswas. Both Rao and Lesh play the cello sitting cross-legged on the floor.

The cello can also be used in [bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_music "Bluegrass music") and [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), with notable players including [Ben Sollee](/wiki/Ben_Sollee "Ben Sollee") of the [Sparrow Quartet](/wiki/Sparrow_Quartet "Sparrow Quartet") and the "Cajun cellist" Sean Grissom, as well as [Vyvienne Long](/wiki/Vyvienne_Long "Vyvienne Long"), who, in addition to her own projects, has played for those of [Damien Rice](/wiki/Damien_Rice "Damien Rice"). Cellists such as [Natalie Haas](/wiki/Natalie_Haas "Natalie Haas"), Abby Newton, and Liz Davis Maxfield have contributed significantly to the use of cello playing in Celtic folk music, often with the cello featured as a primary melodic instrument and employing the skills and techniques of traditional fiddle playing. [Lindsay Mac](/wiki/Lindsay_Mac "Lindsay Mac") is becoming well known for playing the cello like a guitar, with her cover of [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles")' "[Blackbird](/wiki/Blackbird_(Beatles_song) "Blackbird (Beatles song)")".

Canadian electronic music producer Aaron Funk ([Venetian Snares](/wiki/Venetian_Snares "Venetian Snares")), in the piece Szamár Madár (on his 2005 album [Rossz Csillag Alatt Született](/wiki/Rossz_Csillag_Alatt_Sz%C3%BCletett "Rossz Csillag Alatt Született")), extensively samples Edward Elgar's Cello Concerto in E minor, Op. 85.

## Construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Construction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Cello_Parts.jpg/330px-Cello_Parts.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cello_Parts.jpg)

Main parts of the cello

The cello is typically made from carved wood, although other materials such as [carbon fiber](/wiki/Carbon_fiber "Carbon fiber") or aluminum may be used. A traditional cello has a [spruce](/wiki/Spruce "Spruce") top, with [maple](/wiki/Maple "Maple") for the back, sides, and neck. Other woods, such as [poplar](/wiki/Populus "Populus") or [willow](/wiki/Willow "Willow"), are sometimes used for the back and sides. Less expensive cellos frequently have tops and backs made of [laminated wood](/wiki/Plywood "Plywood"). Laminated cellos are widely used in elementary and secondary school orchestras and [youth orchestras](/wiki/Youth_orchestra "Youth orchestra"), because they are much more durable than carved wood cellos (i.e., they are less likely to crack if bumped or dropped) and they are much less expensive.

The top and back are traditionally hand-carved, though less expensive cellos are often machine-produced. The sides, or ribs, are made by heating the wood and bending it around forms. The cello body has a wide top bout, narrow middle formed by two C-bouts, and wide bottom bout, with the [bridge](/wiki/Bridge_(instrument) "Bridge (instrument)") and [F holes](/wiki/F_holes "F holes") just below the middle. The top and back of the cello have a decorative border inlay known as [purfling](/wiki/Purfling "Purfling"). While purfling is attractive, it is also functional: if the instrument is struck, the purfling can prevent cracking of the wood. A crack may form at the rim of the instrument but spreads no further. Without purfling, cracks can spread up or down the top or back. Playing, traveling and the weather all affect the cello and can increase a crack if purfling is not in place.

The fingerboard and pegs on a cello are generally made from [ebony](/wiki/Ebony "Ebony"), as it is strong and does not wear out easily.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Alternative materials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Alternative materials")]

In the late 1920s and early 1930s, the [Aluminum Company of America (Alcoa)](/wiki/Alcoa "Alcoa") as well as German luthier G.A. Pfretzschner produced an unknown number of aluminum cellos (in addition to aluminum double basses and violins).[[21]](#cite_note-21) Cello manufacturer [Luis & Clark](/wiki/Luis_and_Clark "Luis and Clark") constructs cellos from [carbon fibre](/wiki/Carbon_fibre "Carbon fibre"). Carbon fibre instruments are particularly suitable for outdoor playing because of the strength of the material and its resistance to humidity and temperature fluctuations. Luis & Clark has produced over 1,000 cellos, some of which are owned by cellists such as [Yo-Yo Ma](/wiki/Yo-Yo_Ma "Yo-Yo Ma")[[22]](#cite_note-22) and [Josephine van Lier](/wiki/Josephine_van_Lier "Josephine van Lier").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Neck, fingerboard, pegbox, and scroll

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Neck, fingerboard, pegbox, and scroll")]

Above the main body is the carved neck. The neck has a curved cross-section on its underside, which is where the player's thumb runs along the neck during playing. The neck leads to a [pegbox](/wiki/Pegbox "Pegbox") and the [scroll](/wiki/Scroll_(music) "Scroll (music)"), which are all normally carved out of a single piece of wood, usually [maple](/wiki/Maple "Maple"). The [fingerboard](/wiki/Fingerboard "Fingerboard") is glued to the neck and extends over the body of the instrument. The fingerboard is given a curved shape, matching the curve on the bridge. Both the fingerboard and bridge need to be curved so that the performer can bow individual strings. If the cello were to have a flat fingerboard and bridge, as with a typical guitar, the performer would only be able to bow the leftmost and rightmost two strings or bow all the strings. The performer would not be able to play the inner two strings alone.

The [nut](/wiki/Nut_(instrumental) "Nut (instrumental)") is a raised piece of wood, fitted where the fingerboard meets the pegbox, in which the strings rest in shallow slots or grooves to keep them the correct distance apart. The pegbox houses four tapered [tuning pegs](/wiki/Tuning_peg "Tuning peg"), one for each string. The pegs are used to tune the cello by either tightening or loosening the string. The pegs are called "friction pegs", because they maintain their position by friction. The scroll is a traditional ornamental part of the cello and a feature of all other members of the [violin family](/wiki/Violin_family "Violin family"). [Ebony](/wiki/Ebony "Ebony") is usually used for the tuning pegs, fingerboard, and nut, but other hardwoods, such as [boxwood](/wiki/Buxus "Buxus") or [rosewood](/wiki/Rosewood "Rosewood"), can be used. Black fittings on low-cost instruments are often made from inexpensive wood that has been blackened or "ebonized" to look like [ebony](/wiki/Ebony "Ebony"), which is much harder and more expensive. Ebonized parts such as tuning pegs may crack or split, and the black surface of the fingerboard will eventually wear down to reveal the lighter wood underneath.

### Strings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Strings")]

Historically, cello [strings](/wiki/String_(music) "String (music)") had cores made out of [catgut](/wiki/Catgut "Catgut"), which, despite its name, is made from sheep or goat intestines. Most modern strings used in the 2010s are wound with metallic materials like [aluminum](/wiki/Aluminum "Aluminum"), [titanium](/wiki/Titanium "Titanium") and [chromium](/wiki/Chromium "Chromium"). Cellists may mix different types of strings on their instruments. The pitches of the open strings are C, G, D, and A (black note heads in the playing range figure above), unless alternative tuning ([scordatura](/wiki/Scordatura "Scordatura")) is specified by the composer. Some composers (e.g. [Ottorino Respighi](/wiki/Ottorino_Respighi "Ottorino Respighi") in the final movement of *[The Pines of Rome](/wiki/The_Pines_of_Rome "The Pines of Rome")*) ask that the low C be tuned down to a B-flat so that the performer can play a different low note on the lowest open string.

### Tailpiece and endpin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Tailpiece and endpin")]

The [tailpiece](/wiki/Tailpiece "Tailpiece") and [endpin](/wiki/Endpin "Endpin") are found in the lower part of the cello. The tailpiece is the part of the cello to which the "ball ends" of the strings are attached by passing them through holes. The tailpiece is attached to the bottom of the cello. The tailpiece is traditionally made of [ebony](/wiki/Ebony "Ebony") or another hardwood, but can also be made of [plastic](/wiki/Plastic "Plastic") or [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel") on lower-cost instruments. It attaches the strings to the lower end of the cello and can have one or more fine tuners. The fine tuners are used to make smaller adjustments to the pitch of the string. The fine tuners can increase the tension of each string (raising the pitch) or decrease the tension of the string (lowering the pitch). When the performer is putting on a new string, the fine tuner for that string is normally reset to a middle position, and then the peg is turned to bring the string up to pitch. The fine turners are used for subtle, minor adjustments to pitch, such as tuning a cello to the oboe's 440 Hz A note or tuning the cello to a piano.

The endpin or spike is made of wood, metal, or rigid carbon fiber and supports the cello in playing position. The endpin can be retracted into the hollow body of the instrument when the cello is being transported in its case. This makes the cello easier to move about. When the performer wishes to play the cello, the endpin is pulled out to lengthen it. The endpin is locked into the player's preferred length with a screw mechanism. The adjustable nature of endpins enables performers of different ages and body sizes to adjust the endpin length to suit them. In the Baroque period, the cello was held between the calves, as there was no endpin at that time. The endpin was "introduced by [Adrien Servais](/w/index.php?title=Adrien_Servais&action=edit&redlink=1 "Adrien Servais (page does not exist)") c. 1845 to give the instrument greater stability".[[24]](#cite_note-hist-24) Modern endpins are retractable and adjustable; older ones were removed when not in use. (The word "endpin" sometimes also refers to the button of wood located at this place in all instruments in the violin family, but this is usually called "tailpin".[[25]](#cite_note-25)) The sharp tip of the cello's endpin is sometimes capped with a rubber tip that protects the tip from dulling and prevents the cello from slipping on the floor. Many cellists use a rubber pad with a metal cup to keep the tip from slipping on the floor. A number of accessories exist to keep the endpin from slipping; these include ropes that attach to the chair leg and other devices.

### Bridge and f-holes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Bridge and f-holes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Cello_bridge2.JPG/250px-Cello_bridge2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cello_bridge2.JPG)

The bridge of a cello, with a mute (the mute is not in use)

The [bridge](/wiki/Bridge_(instrument) "Bridge (instrument)") holds the strings above the cello and transfers their vibrations to the top of the instrument and the soundpost inside (see below). The bridge is not glued but rather held in place by the tension of the strings. The bridge is usually positioned by the cross point of the "f-hole" (i.e., where the horizontal line occurs in the "f"). The [f-holes](/wiki/Sound-hole "Sound-hole"), named for their shape, are located on either side of the bridge and allow air to move in and out of the instrument as part of the sound-production process. The f-holes also act as access points to the interior of the cello for repairs or maintenance. Sometimes a small length of rubber hose containing a water-soaked sponge, called a Dampit, is inserted through the f-holes and serves as a humidifier. This keeps the wood components of the cello from drying out.

### Internal features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Internal features")]

Internally, the cello has two important features: a [bass bar](/wiki/Bass_bar "Bass bar"), which is glued to the underside of the top of the instrument, and a round wooden [sound post](/wiki/Sound_post "Sound post"), a solid wooden cylinder which is wedged between the top and bottom plates. The bass bar, found under the bass foot of the bridge, serves to support the cello's top and distribute the vibrations from the strings to the body of the instrument. The soundpost, found under the treble side of the bridge, connects the back and front of the cello. Like the bridge, the soundpost is not glued but is kept in place by the tensions of the bridge and strings. Together, the bass bar and sound post transfer the strings' vibrations to the top (front) of the instrument (and to a lesser extent the back), acting as a [diaphragm](/wiki/Diaphragm_(acoustics) "Diaphragm (acoustics)") to produce the instrument's sound.

### Glue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Glue")]

Cellos are constructed and repaired using [hide glue](/wiki/Hide_glue "Hide glue"), which is strong but reversible, allowing for disassembly when needed. Tops may be glued on with diluted glue since some repairs call for the removal of the top. Theoretically, hide glue is weaker than the body's wood, so as the top or back shrinks side-to-side, the glue holding it lets go and the plate does not crack. Cellists repairing cracks in their cello do not use regular [wood glue](/wiki/Wood_glue "Wood glue"), because it cannot be steamed open when a repair has to be made by a [luthier](/wiki/Luthier "Luthier").

### Bow

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Bow")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Bow_hand_Violoncello.jpg/250px-Bow_hand_Violoncello.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bow_hand_Violoncello.jpg)

A cello French bow[[26]](#cite_note-26) *sul ponticello*

Traditionally, [bows](/wiki/Bow_(music) "Bow (music)") are made from [pernambuco](/wiki/Paubrasilia "Paubrasilia") or [brazilwood](/wiki/Brazilwood "Brazilwood"). Both come from the same species of tree (*Caesalpinia echinata*), but Pernambuco, used for higher-quality bows, is the heartwood of the tree and is darker in color than brazilwood (which is sometimes stained to compensate). Pernambuco is a heavy, resinous wood with great elasticity, which makes it an ideal wood for instrument bows. Horsehair is stretched out between the two ends of the bow. The taut horsehair is drawn over the strings, while being held roughly parallel to the bridge and perpendicular to the strings, to produce sound. A small knob is twisted to increase or decrease the tension of the horsehair. The tension on the bow is released when the instrument is not being used. The amount of tension a cellist puts on the bow hair depends on the preferences of the player, the style of music being played, and for students, the preferences of their teacher.

Bows are also made from other materials, such as carbon fibre—stronger than wood—and fiberglass (often used to make inexpensive, lower-quality student bows). An average cello bow is 73 cm (29 in) long (shorter than a violin or viola bow) 3 cm (1.2 in) high (from the frog to the stick) and 1.5 cm (0.59 in) wide. The frog of a cello bow typically has a rounded corner like that of a viola bow, but is wider. A cello bow is roughly 10 g (0.35 oz) heavier than a viola bow, which in turn is roughly 10 g (0.35 oz) heavier than a violin bow.

Bow hair is traditionally [horsehair](/wiki/Horsehair "Horsehair"), though synthetic hair, in varying colors, is also used. Prior to playing, the musician tightens the bow by turning a screw to pull the frog (the part of the bow under the hand) back and increase the tension of the hair. [Rosin](/wiki/Rosin "Rosin") is applied by the player to make the hair sticky. Bows need to be re-haired periodically. [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque")-style (1600–1750) cello bows were much thicker and were formed with a larger outward arch than modern cello bows. The inward arch of a modern cello bow produces greater tension, which in turn produces a louder sound.

The cello bow has also been used to play [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar"). Jimmy Page pioneered its application on tracks such as "[Dazed and Confused](/wiki/Dazed_and_Confused_(Jake_Holmes_song) "Dazed and Confused (Jake Holmes song)")". The [post-rock](/wiki/Post-rock "Post-rock") [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelanders "Icelanders") band [Sigur Rós](/wiki/Sigur_R%C3%B3s "Sigur Rós")'s lead singer often plays guitar using a cello bow.

In 1989, the German cellist [Michael Bach](/wiki/Michael_Bach_(musician) "Michael Bach (musician)") began developing a [curved bow](/wiki/Curved_bow "Curved bow"), encouraged by [John Cage](/wiki/John_Cage "John Cage"), [Dieter Schnebel](/wiki/Dieter_Schnebel "Dieter Schnebel"), Mstislav Rostropovich and [Luigi Colani](/wiki/Luigi_Colani "Luigi Colani"): and since then many pieces have been composed especially for it. This curved bow (*BACH.Bow*) is a convex curved bow which, unlike the ordinary bow, renders possible polyphonic playing on the various strings of the instrument. The BACH.Bow is particularly well-suited to the solo repertoire for violin and cello by J. S. Bach; which requires both polyphonic and monophonic playing.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Michael_Bach%2C_Cello_with_BACH.Bow.jpg/250px-Michael_Bach%2C_Cello_with_BACH.Bow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Bach,_Cello_with_BACH.Bow.jpg)

Michael Bach, cello with [BACH.Bow](/wiki/Curved_bow "Curved bow")

## Physics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Physics")]

### Physical aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Physical aspects")]

When a string is bowed or [plucked](/wiki/Pizzicato "Pizzicato"), it vibrates and moves the air around it, producing sound waves. Because the string is quite thin, not much air is moved by the string itself, and consequently, if the string was not mounted on a hollow body, the sound would be weak. In acoustic stringed instruments such as the cello, this lack of volume is solved by mounting the vibrating string on a larger hollow wooden body. The vibrations are transmitted to the larger body, which can move more air and produce a louder sound. Different designs of the instrument produce variations in the instrument's vibrational patterns and thus change the character of the sound produced.[[27]](#cite_note-27) A string's fundamental pitch can be adjusted by changing its stiffness, which depends on tension and length. Tightening a string stiffens it by increasing both the outward forces along its length and the net forces it experiences during a distortion.[[28]](#cite_note-Bloomfield,_Louis_A_2001._pp_241-244-28) A cello can be tuned by adjusting the tension of its strings, by turning the tuning pegs mounted on its pegbox and tension adjusters (fine tuners) on the tailpiece.

A string's length also affects its fundamental pitch. Shortening a string stiffens it by increasing its curvature during a distortion and subjecting it to larger net forces. Shortening the string also reduces its mass, but does not alter the mass per unit length, and it is the latter ratio rather than the total mass which governs the frequency. The string vibrates in a standing wave whose speed of propagation is given by ![{\textstyle {\sqrt {\frac {T}{m}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1f8cceda8e712efdf53d2be57a8f4353b8585a03), where T is the tension and m is the mass per unit length; there is a node at either end of the vibrating length, and thus the vibrating length l is half a wavelength. Since the frequency of any wave is equal to the speed divided by the wavelength, we have ![{\textstyle \mathrm {frequency} ={\frac {1}{2l}}\cdot {\sqrt {\frac {T}{m}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f8c00cf7827331f53d7e50013d2947e3df729296). (Some writers, including Muncaster (cited below), use the Greek letter μ in place of m.) Thus shortening a string increases the frequency, and thus the pitch. Because of this effect, you can raise and change the pitch of a string by pressing it against the fingerboard in the cello's neck and effectively shortening it.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Likewise strings with less mass per unit length, if under the same tension, will have a higher frequency and thus higher pitch than more massive strings. This is a prime reason the different strings on all string instruments have different fundamental pitches, with the lightest strings having the highest pitches.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Spectrogram_Cello_D_Arpeggio.png/120px-Spectrogram_Cello_D_Arpeggio.png)](/wiki/File:Spectrogram_Cello_D_Arpeggio.png)

[Spectrogram](/wiki/Spectrogram "Spectrogram") of a D chord arpeggiated on the cello. Yellow bands at the same level indicate the same harmonics excited by the bowing of different notes. Notes played from left to right: D–F♯–A–F♯–D.

A played note of E or F♯ has a frequency that is often very close to the natural resonating frequency of the body of the instrument, and if the problem is not addressed this can set the body into near resonance. This may cause an unpleasant sudden amplification of this pitch, and additionally a loud beating sound results from the interference produced between these nearby frequencies; this is known as the "[wolf tone](/wiki/Wolf_tone "Wolf tone")" because it is an unpleasant growling sound. The wood resonance appears to be split into two frequencies by the driving force of the sounding string. These two periodic resonances beat with each other. This wolf tone must be eliminated or significantly reduced for the cello to play the nearby notes with a pleasant tone. This can be accomplished by modifying the cello front plate, attaching a wolf eliminator (a metal cylinder or a rubber cylinder encased in metal), or moving the soundpost.[[30]](#cite_note-Berg,_Richard_E_2005._pp_314,_329-30)

When a string is bowed or plucked to produce a note, the fundamental note is accompanied by higher frequency overtones. Each sound has a particular recipe of frequencies that combine to make the total sound.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Playing technique

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Playing technique")]

Main article: [Cello technique](/wiki/Cello_technique "Cello technique")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Cello_KSG_9829_PK.jpg/250px-Cello_KSG_9829_PK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cello_KSG_9829_PK.jpg)

A cellist

Playing the cello is done while seated with the instrument supported on the floor by the endpin. The right hand bows (or sometimes plucks) the strings to sound the notes. The left-hand fingertips stop the strings along their length, determining the pitch of each fingered note. Stopping the string closer to the bridge results in a higher-pitched sound because the vibrating string length has been shortened. On the contrary, a string stopped closer to the tuning pegs produces a lower sound. In the *neck* positions (which use just less than half of the fingerboard, nearest the top of the instrument), the thumb rests on the back of the neck, some people use their thumb as a marker of their position; in *[thumb position](/wiki/Thumb_position "Thumb position")* (a general name for notes on the remainder of the fingerboard) the thumb usually rests alongside the fingers on the string. Then, the side of the thumb is used to play notes. The fingers are normally held curved with each knuckle bent, with the fingertips in contact with the string. If a finger is required on two (or more) strings at once to play perfect fifths (in double stops or chords), it is used flat. The contact point can move slightly away from the nail to the finger's pad in slower or more expressive playing, allowing a fuller vibrato.

[Vibrato](/wiki/Vibrato "Vibrato") is a small oscillation in the pitch of a note, usually considered an expressive [technique](/wiki/Cello_technique "Cello technique"). The closer towards the bridge the note is, the smaller the oscillation needed to create the effect. [Harmonics](/wiki/Harmonics "Harmonics") played on the cello fall into two classes; natural and artificial. Natural harmonics are produced by lightly touching (but not depressing) the string at certain places and then bowing (or, rarely, plucking) the string. For example, the halfway point of the string will produce a harmonic that is one octave above the unfingered (open) string. Natural harmonics only produce notes that are part of the [harmonic series](/wiki/Harmonic_series_(music) "Harmonic series (music)") on a particular string. Artificial harmonics (also called false harmonics or stopped harmonics), in which the player depresses the string fully with one finger while touching the same string lightly with another finger, can produce any note above middle C.
[Glissando](/wiki/Glissando "Glissando") (Italian for "sliding") is an effect achieved by sliding the finger up or down the fingerboard without releasing the string. This causes the pitch to rise and fall smoothly, without separate, discernible steps.

In cello playing, the bow is much like the breath of a [wind instrument](/wiki/Aerophone "Aerophone") player. Arguably, it is a major factor in the expressiveness of the playing. The right hand holds the bow and controls the duration and character of the notes. In general, the bow is drawn across the strings roughly halfway between the end of the [fingerboard](/wiki/Fingerboard "Fingerboard") and the bridge, in a direction perpendicular to the strings; however, the player may wish to move the bow's point of contact higher or lower depending on the desired sound. The bow is held and manipulated with all five fingers of the right hand, with the thumb opposite the fingers and closer to the cellist's body. Tone production and volume of sound depend on a combination of several [factors](/wiki/Cello_technique "Cello technique"). The four most important ones are *weight* applied to the string, the *angle* of the bow on the string, bow *speed*, and the *point of contact* of the bow hair with the string (sometimes abbreviated WASP).

[Double stops](/wiki/Double_stop "Double stop") involve the playing of two notes simultaneously. Two strings are fingered at once, and the bow is drawn to sound them both. Often, in [pizzicato](/wiki/Pizzicato "Pizzicato") playing, the string is plucked directly with the fingers or thumb of the right hand. However, the strings may be plucked with a finger of the left hand in certain advanced pieces, either so that the cellist can play bowed notes on another string along with pizzicato notes or because the speed of the piece would not allow the player sufficient time to pluck with the right hand. In [musical notation](/wiki/Musical_notation "Musical notation"), pizzicato is often abbreviated as "pizz." The position of the hand in pizzicato is commonly slightly over the fingerboard and away from the bridge.

A player using the [col legno](/wiki/Col_legno "Col legno") technique strikes or rubs the strings with the wood of the bow rather than the hair. In [spiccato](/wiki/Spiccato "Spiccato") playing, the bow still moves in a horizontal motion on the string but is allowed to bounce, generating a lighter, somewhat more percussive sound. In [staccato](/wiki/Staccato "Staccato"), the player moves the bow a small distance and stops it on the string, making a short sound, the rest of the written duration being taken up by silence.
[Legato](/wiki/Legato "Legato") is a technique in which notes are smoothly connected without breaks. It is indicated by a slur (curved line) above or below – depending on their position on the staff – the notes of the passage that is to be played legato.

*Sul ponticello* ("on the bridge") refers to bowing closer to (or nearly on) the bridge, while *sul tasto* ("on the fingerboard") calls for bowing nearer to (or over) the end of the fingerboard. At its extreme, sul ponticello produces a harsh, shrill sound with emphasis on overtones and high harmonics. In contrast, sul tasto produces a more flute-like sound that emphasizes the note's fundamental frequency and produces softened overtones. Composers have used both techniques, particularly in an orchestral setting, for special sounds and effects.

## Sizes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Sizes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Full_size_and_fractional_cello.jpg/250px-Full_size_and_fractional_cello.jpg)](/wiki/File:Full_size_and_fractional_cello.jpg)

1⁄8 size cello with full size cello

Standard-sized cellos are referred to as "full-size" or "4⁄4" but are also made in smaller (fractional) sizes, including 15⁄16, 7⁄8, 3⁄4, 1⁄2, 1⁄4, 1⁄8, 1⁄10, and 1⁄16. The fractions refer to volume rather than length, so a 1⁄2 size cello is much longer than half the length of a full size. The smaller cellos are identical to standard cellos in construction, range, and usage, but are simply scaled-down for the benefit of children and shorter adults.

Cellos in sizes larger than 4⁄4 do exist, and cellists with unusually large hands may require such a non-standard instrument. Cellos made before c. 1700 tended to be considerably larger than those made and commonly played today. Around 1680, changes in string-making technology made it possible to play lower-pitched notes on shorter strings. The cellos of [Stradivari](/wiki/Stradivari "Stradivari"), for example, can be clearly divided into two models: the style made before 1702, characterized by larger instruments (of which only three exist in their original size and configuration), and the style made during and after 1707, when Stradivari began making smaller cellos. This later model is the design most commonly used by modern luthiers. The [scale length](/wiki/Scale_length_(string_instruments) "Scale length (string instruments)") of a 4⁄4 cello is about 70 cm (27+1⁄2 in). The new size offered fuller tonal projection and a greater range of expression. The instrument in this form was able to contribute to more pieces musically and offered the possibility of greater physical dexterity for the player to develop technique.[[32]](#cite_note-size-32)

| Approximate dimensions for 4⁄4 size cello[[33]](#cite_note-33) | Average size |
| --- | --- |

| Back length excluding half-round where neck joins | 75.4 cm (29.7 in) |
| Upper bouts (shoulders) | 34.0 cm (13.4 in) |

| Bridge height | 8.9 cm (3.5 in) |

| Rib depth at hips including edges | 12.7 cm (5.0 in) |

## Accessories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Accessories")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Rosins.JPG/250px-Rosins.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rosins.JPG)

Rosin is applied to bow hair to increase the friction of the bow on the strings.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Wolf_tone_eliminator.jpg/250px-Wolf_tone_eliminator.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wolf_tone_eliminator.jpg)

A brass wolf tone eliminator typically placed on the G string (second string from the left) of a cello, between the bridge and the tailpiece. (The black rubber piece on the D string (third from the left) is a [mute](/wiki/Mute_(music) "Mute (music)").)

There are many accessories for the cello.

* Cases are used to protect the cello and bow (or multiple bows).
* [Rosin](/wiki/Rosin "Rosin"), made from [resins](/wiki/Resin "Resin") [tapped](/wiki/Resin_extraction "Resin extraction") from [conifers](/wiki/Pinophyta "Pinophyta"), is applied to the bow hair to increase the effectiveness of the friction, grip or bite, and allow proper sound production. Rosin may have additives to modify the friction such as beeswax, gold, silver or tin. Commonly, rosins are classified as either dark or light, referring to color.
* [Endpin](/wiki/Endpin "Endpin") stops or straps (tradenames include Rock stop and Black Hole) keep the cello from sliding if the endpin does not have a rubber piece on the end, or if a floor is particularly slippery.
* [Wolf tone eliminators](/wiki/Wolf_tone_eliminator "Wolf tone eliminator") are placed on cello strings between the tailpiece and the bridge to eliminate acoustic anomalies known as [wolf tones](/wiki/Wolf_tone "Wolf tone") or "wolfs".
* [Mutes](/wiki/Mute_(music)#String "Mute (music)") are used to change the sound of the cello by adding mass and stiffness to the bridge. They alter the [overtone](/wiki/Overtone "Overtone") structure, modifying the timbre and reducing the overall volume of sound produced by the instrument.
* [Metronomes](/wiki/Metronome "Metronome") provide a steady [tempo](/wiki/Tempo "Tempo") by sounding out a certain number of beats per minute. This tool is often used to instill a sense of rhythm into a musician. It acts as a mirror for rhythmic stability, allowing the musician to analyze where they rush or drag a tempo.
* Fine tuners, located on the tailpiece, allow the cello to be tuned easily and with greater accuracy.

## Famous instruments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cello&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Famous instruments")]

See also: [Category:Individual cellos](/wiki/Category:Individual_cellos "Category:Individual cellos")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Servais_Stradivarius.jpg/250px-Servais_Stradivarius.jpg)](/wiki/File:Servais_Stradivarius.jpg)

The Servais Stradivarius is preserved in the [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution")'s [National Museum of American History](/wiki/National_Museum_of_American_History "National Museum of American History")

Specific instruments are famous (or become famous) for a variety of reasons. An instrument's notability may arise from its age, the fame of its maker, its physical appearance, its acoustic properties, and its use by notable performers. The most famous instruments are generally known for all of these things. The most highly prized instruments are now collector's items and are priced beyond the reach of most musicians. These instruments are typically owned by some kind of organization or investment group, which may loan the instrument to a notable performer. For example, the [Davidov Stradivarius](/wiki/Davidov_Stradivarius "Davidov Stradivarius"), which is currently in the possession of one of the most widely known living cellists, Yo-Yo Ma, is actually owned by the Vuitton Foundation.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Some notable cellos:

* the "King", by [Andrea Amati](/wiki/Andrea_Amati "Andrea Amati"), is one of the oldest known cellos, built between 1538 and 1560—it is in the collection of the [National Music Museum](/wiki/National_Music_Museum "National Music Museum") in [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota").[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* [Servais Stradivarius](/wiki/Servais_Stradivarius "Servais Stradivarius") is in the collection of the [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution"), Washington DC.
* [Batta-Piatigorsky Stradivarius](/wiki/Batta-Piatigorsky_Stradivarius "Batta-Piatigorsky Stradivarius"), played by Gregor Piatigorsky.
* [Davidov Stradivarius](/wiki/Davidov_Stradivarius "Davidov Stradivarius"), played by Jacqueline du Pré, currently played by Yo-Yo Ma.
* [Barjansky Stradivarius](/wiki/Barjansky_Stradivarius "Barjansky Stradivarius"), played by [Julian Lloyd Webber](/wiki/Julian_Lloyd_Webber "Julian Lloyd Webber").
* [Bonjour Stradivarius](/wiki/Bonjour_Stradivarius "Bonjour Stradivarius"), played by [Soo Bae](/wiki/Soo_Bae "Soo Bae").
* [Paganini-Ladenburg Stradivarius](/wiki/Paganini_Quartet "Paganini Quartet"), played by Clive Greensmith of the [Tokyo String Quartet](/wiki/Tokyo_String_Quartet "Tokyo String Quartet").
* [Duport Stradivarius](/wiki/Duport_Stradivarius "Duport Stradivarius"), formerly played by Mstislav Rostropovich.
* Piatti Stradivarius, 1720, played by [Carlos Prieto](/wiki/Carlos_Prieto_(cellist) "Carlos Prieto (cellist)")
